analogy: Think of "Colonialism and the Countryside" through the chapter's own opening images: 227 In this chapter you will see what colonial rule meant to those who lived in the countryside. You will meet the zamindars of Bengal, travel to the Rajmahal hills where the Paharias and the Santhals lived, and then move west to the Deccan. You... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Colonialism and the Countryside is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 227 In this chapter you will see what colonial rule meant to those who lived in the countryside.

The text explains that you will meet the zamindars of Bengal, travel to the Rajmahal hills where the Paharias and the Santhals lived, and then move west to the Deccan.

The text explains that you will look at the way the English East India Company (E.I.C.) established its raj in the countryside, implemented its revenue policies, what these policies meant to different sections of people, and how they changed everyday lives.

The text explains that laws introduced by the state have consequences for people: they determine to an extent who grows richer and who poorer, who acquires new land and who loses the land they have lived on, where peasants go when they need money.

The text explains that as you will see, however, people were not only subject to the working of laws, they also resisted the law by acting according to what they believed to be just.

The text explains that in doing so people defined the way in which laws operated, thereby modifying their consequences.

The text explains that you will also come to know about the sources that tell us about these histories, and the problems historians face in interpreting them.

The text explains that you will read about revenue records and surveys, journals and accounts left by surveyors and travellers, and reports produced by enquiry commissions.

The text explains that 9.1 Cotton being carried from the village to the mandi, Illustrated London News, 20 April 1861 Colonialism and the Countryside Explor Explor Explor Explor Exploring Of ing Of ing Of ing Of ing Off f f f ficial Ar icial Ar icial Ar icial Ar icial Arc c c c chiv hiv hiv hiv hives es es es es THEME NINE

The text explains that bengal and the Zamindars As you know, colonial rule was first established in Bengal.

The text explains that it is here that the earliest attempts were made to reorder rural society and establish a new regime of land rights and a new revenue system.

The text explains that let us see what happened in Bengal in the early years of Company (E.I.C.) rule.

The text explains that 1.1 An auction in Burdwan In 1797 there was an auction in Burdwan (present- day Bardhaman).

The text explains that a number of mahals (estates) held by the Raja of Burdwan were being sold.

The text explains that the Permanent Settlement had come into operation in 1793.

The text explains that the East India Company had fixed the revenue that each zamindar had to pay.

The text explains that officials felt that agriculture, trade and the revenue resources of the state could all be developed by encouraging investment in agriculture.

The text explains that ryots in Bengal did not always cultivate the land directly, but leased it out to under-ryots.

The text explains that unlike zamindars who often lived in urban areas, jotedars were located in the villages and exercised direct control over a considerable section of poor villagers.

The text explains that faced with an exorbitantly high revenue demand and possible auction of their estates, they devised ways of surviving the pressures.

The text explains that the Source 1 Ü Describe the ways in which the jotedars resisted the authority of the zamindars. Ü Read the text accompanying Fig.9.5 carefully and insert the following terms in appropriate places along the arrows: rent, revenue, interest, loan, produce Fig.

The text explains that to the British, extension of settled agriculture was necessary to enlarge the sources of land revenue, produce crops for export, and establish the basis of a settled, ordered society.

The text explains that 9.9 A view of Jangal territory, painted by William Hodges Here you can see the forested low hills and the rocky upper ranges, nowhere actually above 2,000 feet.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 240 2.2 The Santhals: Pioneer settlers At the end of 1810, Buchanan crossed Ganjuria Pahar, which was part of the Rajmahal ranges, passed through the rocky country beyond, and reached a village.

The text explains that i think its beauty and riches might be made equal to almost any in the universe.” The soil here was rocky but “uncommonly fine”, and nowhere had Buchanan seen finer tobacco and mustard.

The text explains that restricted from moving down to the lower hills and valleys, they were confined to the dry interior and to the more barren and rocky upper hills.

The text explains that as the Company consolidated its power and expanded its commerce, it looked for natural resources it could control and exploit.

The text explains that it surveyed landscapes and revenue sources, organised voyages of discovery, and sent its geologists and geographers, its botanists and medical men to collect information.

The text explains that everywhere Buchanan went, he obsessively observed the stones and rocks and the different strata and layers of soil.

The text explains that he searched for minerals and stones that were commercially valuable, he recorded all signs of iron ore and mica, granite and saltpetre.

The text explains that the rocks near Kaduya Buchanan’s journal is packed with observations like the following: About a mile farther on, (I) came to a low ledge of rocks without any evident strata; it is a small grained granite with reddish feldspar, with quartz and black mica … More than half a mile from thence, I came to another rock not stratified, and consisting of very fine- grained granite with yellowish feldspar, whitish quartz and black mica.

The text explains that source 4 Buchanan on the Santhals Buchanan wrote: They are very clever in clearing new lands, but live meanly.

The text explains that 245 On clearance and settled cultivation Passing through one village in the lower Rajmahal hills, Buchanan wrote: The view of the country is exceedingly fine, the cultivation, especially the narrow valleys of rice winding in all directions, the cleared lands with scattered trees, and the rocky hills are in perfection; all that is wanted is some appearance of progress in the area and a vastly extended and improved cultivation, of which the country is highly susceptible.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 246 Source 7 Ü The words and terms used by a writer often tell us something about his or her prejudices.

The text explains that read Source 7 carefully and pick out the terms that indicate any prejudices of the writer.

The text explains that dECCAN RIOTS COMMISSION Source 6 A sahukar was someone who acted as both a moneylender and a trader. moneylenders and grain dealers.

The text explains that keen on expanding its financial resources, the colonial government had to think of ways to maximise its land revenue.

The text explains that unlike the Bengal system, the revenue was directly settled with the ryot.

The text explains that what would happen if this source was cut off ?

The text explains that troubled by this question, they eagerly looked for alternative sources of supply.

The text explains that when the American Civil War broke out in 1861, a wave of panic spread through cotton circles in Britain.

The text explains that moneylending was certainly widespread before colonial rule and moneylenders were often powerful Source 8 Ü Explain the complaints that the ryot is making in the petition.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 254 Source 10 the law around, forcing the ryot to sign a new bond every three years.

The text explains that in petitions that the Deccan Riots Commission collected, ryots described how this process worked (see Source 10) and how moneylenders used a variety of other means to short-change the ryot: they refused to give receipts when loans were repaid, entered fictitious figures in bonds, acquired the peasants’ harvest at low prices, and ultimately took over peasants’ property.

The text explains that this report, referred to as the Deccan Riots Report, provides historians with a range of sources for the study of the riot.

The text explains that in looking at such sources we have to again remember that they are official sources and reflect official concerns and interpretations of events.

The text explains that official reports, thus, are invaluable sources for the reconstruction of history.

The text explains that but they have to be always read with care and juxtaposed with evidence culled from newspapers, unofficial accounts, legal records and, where possible, oral sources. Ü Discuss...

The text explains that what are the problems of using official sources in writing about the history of peasants?

The text explains that highlight the ways in which historians can use such texts.

The text explains that in the region where you live, talk to the older people within a rural community and visit the fields they now cultivate.

The text explains that find out what they produce, how they earn their livelihoods, what their parents did, what their sons and daughters do now, and how their lives have changed over the last 75 years.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III, Chapter 1: Colonialism and the Countryside

schemes.name: The Fifth Report
schemes.point: The Fifth Report was one such report produced by a Select Committee. (NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III Ch1 pg 7)

schemes.name: On
schemes.point: On the request of the Government of Bengal, he undertook detailed surveys of the areas under the jurisdiction of the British East India Company. (NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III Ch1 pg 9)

schemes.name: In
schemes.point: In the 1770s the British embarked on a brutal policy of extermination, hunting the Paharias down and killing them. (NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III Ch1 pg 12)

schemes.name: Then
schemes.point: Then, by the 1780s, Augustus Cleveland, the Collector of Bhagalpur, proposed a policy of pacification. (NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III Ch1 pg 12)

schemes.name: Being
schemes.point: Being in the pay of the colonial government, they came to be perceived as subordinate employees or stipendiary chiefs. (NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III Ch1 pg 12)

prelims_traps: Do not treat THEME and NINE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Colonialism and the Countryside; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Colonialism and the Countryside through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use THEME and NINE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Colonialism and the Countryside, which statement is supported by the source text about 1.2 The problem of unpaid revenue?
options: The estates of those who failed to pay were to be auctioned to recover the revenue.

Numerous purchasers came to the auction and the estates were sold to the highest bidder.
But the Collector soon discovered a strange twist to the tale.
